student_response:
In the decades before 1980, the national parties were broad coalitions that often included voters with different views. Economic problems and social conflicts during the 1970s made many voters less satisfied with that arrangement. From 1980 to 2020, national political polarization grew mainly because voters became more clearly divided by ideology and because partisan media gave each side a seperate version of political events. These causes made compromise seem like giving in to an enemy instead of normal goverment work.

Ronald Reagan's 1980 election helped create stronger ideological sorting. His campaign connected lower taxes, less federal regulation, a larger military, and traditional social values with the Republican Party. Conservative voters who had once been spread across both parties increasingly gathered in one party, while liberal voters moved more firmly toward the Democrats. This made elections into contests between clearer ideological sides. Politicians then had a reason to use sharper language because their most loyal voters expected them to defend the party position, so cooperation across party lines became less common.

Fox News also increased polarization after it began broadcasting in 1994. Its programs presented politics through a conservative viewpoint and focused heavily on conflict with Democratic leaders. Viewers could repeatedly hear arguements that confirmed what they already believed, rather than sharing the same information with people from the other party. Later internet and social media habits sped up this same division by rewarding angry messages and allowing users to stay around similar opinions. As ideological sorting separated the parties and partisan media kept their voters in opposing information groups, national poltical disagreement became more intense and constant by 2020.
